questions.text: The concept of 'Human Life Value' (HLV) was invented by which famous professor?
questions.explanation: Prof. S.S. Huebner created the concept of Human Life Value (HLV) to calculate the economic value of a human life based on their future earning capacity.

questions.text: What does the 'Level Premium System' in life insurance mean?
options: Premium increases every year as age increases
Premium remains the same (fixed) throughout the entire policy term
Premium decreases as the person gets older
Premium changes depending on market inflation
questions.explanation: In a level premium system, the insurance company averages out the risk cost so that the policyholder pays a constant, fixed amount of premium every year for the entire term.

questions.text: In life insurance, which of the following represents the 'asset' being insured?
options: The physical house of the person
The bank balance of the person
The economic value / income generating capacity of the human life
The physical body of the insured
questions.explanation: Unlike general insurance which protects physical assets like cars or houses, life insurance protects the 'economic value' (future earning capacity) of the human life.

questions.text: Why is an 'investment element' or 'cash reserve' automatically built into the Level Premium system?
options: To give high bonuses to agents
To reduce the tax burden of the company
Because IRDAI legally mandates a savings account for everyone
To compensate for the higher mortality risk in later years without increasing the premium
questions.explanation: In early years, level premium collects more than the actual risk cost. This excess is saved as a 'reserve' (cash value) to pay for the higher risk of death when the person gets much older, keeping the premium level.

questions.text: How is Human Life Value (HLV) generally calculated in practical terms?
options: By calculating the present value of future net earnings of the person
By adding the total loans taken by the person
By multiplying the person's age with their weight
By checking the current bank balance of the proposer
questions.explanation: HLV is calculated by taking the net income a person brings to their family and estimating its present financial value over their remaining working years.

questions.text: When calculating the net income for HLV, which component is strictly deducted from the gross income?
options: Personal self-maintenance expenses (like the person's own food/clothing)
House rent paid by the family
questions.explanation: To calculate HLV, we deduct the amount the earner spends on themselves (self-maintenance) because if they die, that expense also stops. The remainder is what the family actually loses.

questions.text: Unlike a fire insurance contract, a life insurance contract is fundamentally considered a contract of:
questions.explanation: Life insurance is a 'Valued' or Fixed Benefit contract. It pays a pre-agreed guaranteed amount (Sum Assured) upon death, unlike general insurance which pays only exact indemnity for the loss.

questions.text: Which of the following risk management techniques best describes the mechanism of purchasing life insurance?
questions.explanation: By buying insurance and paying a premium, the policyholder 'transfers' the heavy financial risk of premature death to the insurance company.

questions.text: Under the 'Natural Premium' system (not the level premium system), what happens to the life insurance premium as a person's age increases?
options: The premium increases every year because the risk of death increases
The premium decreases to help senior citizens
The premium becomes zero after 10 years
questions.explanation: In a natural premium system, the premium matches the exact risk for that specific age. Since mortality risk goes up with age, the premium would increase every single year.

questions.text: What happens in the early years of a 'Level Premium' life insurance policy?
options: The premium charged is less than the actual risk cost
The premium charged is more than the actual risk cost
The premium exactly matches the risk cost
No premium is charged at all
questions.explanation: To keep the premium 'level' for the whole term, the company charges more than the actual cost of risk during the early (younger) years and saves the excess.

questions.text: The excess money collected by the insurer in the early years of a level premium policy forms what is known as the:
questions.explanation: The overcharged amount in early years is invested by the insurer to create a 'Reserve'. This reserve builds the 'Cash Value' of the policy, which enables features like policy loans and surrender values.

questions.text: A person earns Rs. 5 Lakhs a year. He spends Rs. 1 Lakh entirely on his own personal expenses. What amount is used as the base for calculating his family's financial loss (HLV)?
questions.explanation: Gross Income (5 Lakhs) minus Self-maintenance expenses (1 Lakh) equals the Net Income (4 Lakhs) that actually supports the family. This 4 Lakhs is the base for HLV calculation.

questions.text: Which basic principle dictates that many people exposed to a similar risk contribute to a common fund to pay the few who suffer a loss?
questions.explanation: Mutuality or Risk Pooling is the core scientific mechanism of all insurance. The losses of a few are shared by the contributions of many.

questions.text: Why can't the legal principle of strict 'Indemnity' be applied to life insurance contracts?
options: Because life insurance companies don't have enough money to indemnify
Because the exact financial value of a human life cannot be precisely measured or replaced
Because IRDAI explicitly prevents it to protect agents
Because life insurance is a short-term contract
questions.explanation: Indemnity means restoring the exact financial loss. Since no one can put an exact monetary price on a human life to 'restore' it, life insurance pays a pre-decided fixed sum assured instead.

questions.text: In a pure 'Term Insurance' policy, if the insured survives the policy term, what amount is usually payable?
questions.explanation: A pure term insurance policy only provides death risk cover. It does not have a savings element. If the insured survives, the policy expires and nothing is paid back.

questions.text: As a whole life policy progresses towards maturity and its Cash Value (Reserve) increases, what happens to the 'Amount at Risk' for the insurance company?
questions.explanation: Amount at Risk = Sum Assured MINUS Cash Value. As the cash value reserve grows larger over time, the actual net amount the company has to pay out of its own pocket (the risk amount) decreases.

questions.text: The principle of 'Diversification' used by insurance companies to invest funds is best described by which common phrase?
options: Don't put all your eggs in one basket
questions.explanation: Insurance companies use diversification (spreading investments across different sectors like government bonds, equity, real estate) to minimize risk, akin to 'not putting all eggs in one basket'.

questions.text: For an insurance contract to be legally valid, the possibility of the risk occurring must be:
options: Completely controlled by the insured person
questions.explanation: Insurance only covers fortuitous (accidental and unpredictable) events. If a loss is deliberate or 100% certain to happen at a known time, it cannot be insured.

questions.text: What is the main disadvantage of the 'Natural Premium' method for life insurance that led to the creation of the Level Premium system?
options: It is declared illegal by IRDAI
It becomes unaffordable at older ages when insurance is needed the most
It does not allow the company to pay death claims
It requires the policyholder to take monthly medical tests
questions.explanation: Because Natural Premium increases with age, it becomes incredibly expensive when a person reaches old age, forcing them to drop the policy right when they are most likely to die.

questions.text: Who among the following would typically have the highest Human Life Value (HLV), assuming their current monthly income is exactly the same?
options: Age does not affect the calculation of HLV
questions.explanation: A 30-year-old has many more working years left until retirement compared to a 60-year-old. Therefore, the present value of their future earnings (HLV) will be significantly higher.

questions.text: Which of the following elements strongly distinguishes Life Insurance from most General Insurance contracts?
options: It is a long-term contract and often includes a savings/investment element
It strictly follows the principle of indemnity
It is usually valid for only one year and requires annual renewal
It does not require insurable interest at any stage
questions.explanation: General insurance (like motor or fire) is typically a 1-year pure risk contract. Life insurance is a long-term contract that bundles both risk protection and a cash-value (savings) element.

questions.text: The creation of a 'Reserve' in life insurance is a direct and necessary result of which pricing method?
questions.explanation: The Level Premium method intentionally overcharges in the early years. This overcharge must be set aside to cover future deficits, thus creating the 'Reserve'.

questions.text: If Mr. X dies prematurely, his family will lose his future income. This potential financial loss forms the core basis of his:
questions.explanation: The concept of HLV was specifically designed to quantify this exact loss—the economic value of the income that a breadwinner would have earned for their family.

questions.text: Most traditional life insurance policies provide a combination of two distinct elements. What are they?
questions.explanation: Thanks to the Level Premium system, traditional life policies (like Endowment) bundle two things: protection against the risk of death, and a savings element that builds cash value over time.

questions.text: In the technical context of life insurance, what does 'Amount at Risk' mean for the insurer?
options: The total premium paid by the customer till date
The difference between the total Sum Assured and the accumulated Cash Value (Reserve)
The maximum penalty imposed by the regulator
The sum assured divided by the age of the policyholder
questions.explanation: The actual financial risk to the company decreases over time because they use the accumulated cash value to pay a part of the death claim. The 'Amount at Risk' is strictly the Sum Assured minus the Cash Value.
